findings.description: The original two-column page layout is not preserved in the HTML.
findings.evidence: In the PDF, page 1 presents two separate side-by-side tables divided by a central vertical graphic, with the left table showing FY 2013 summary data and the right table showing FY 2012 vs FY 2013 change data. In the HTML, these are stacked vertically in a single-column flow.

findings.description: Reading order differs because the right-hand column content is moved below the left-hand column content.
findings.evidence: A sighted user reading the PDF encounters both left and right tables at the same visual level. In the HTML, the full left table is read first, then the second report date and right table appear later, changing the visual reading sequence.

findings.description: The central vertical graphic/divider from the PDF is omitted in the HTML.
findings.evidence: The PDF includes a narrow striped vertical divider between the two columns. The HTML has no corresponding graphic, leaving only stacked tables.

findings.description: Text styling and emphasis are simplified in the HTML compared with the PDF.
findings.evidence: The PDF uses stronger visual distinction with colored headings, colored numeric text, and italicized report run dates. The HTML retains the text content but flattens much of the color/emphasis and appears more uniformly styled.

findings.description: The HTML includes fabricated styling/theme text and branding metadata not present in the source PDF.
findings.evidence: HTML contains CSS/branding text such as "Digital Commons NC — Standalone Theme CSS", font imports, and variables like "--nc-font", "--nc-navy", "--nc-blue". These do not appear in the PDF image, which only shows the report content.

findings.description: The HTML adds a fabricated visible heading not present as such in the PDF structure.
findings.evidence:
HTML page header uses "

Active Reported Payment Error Rates

". In the PDF, this text appears as a page title on the report, but not as a semantic HTML heading; however this is generally acceptable. The more substantive fabrication is the CSS branding and wrapper metadata noted above.

findings.description: One table header is transcribed inaccurately, changing the source label.
findings.evidence: PDF right-hand table header reads "FY 2012 Cumulative Oct - Sep /2" only if interpreted from the OCR, but visually the PDF shows "FY 2012 Cumulative Oct - Sep /2" with line breaks and likely a formatting artifact. HTML preserves this odd text literally; no clear data loss is evident. However, the slash formatting appears suspicious and may reflect OCR uncertainty rather than faithful labeling.

findings.description: Heading hierarchy is only partially faithful to the PDF structure and introduces a nested <main> plus semantic headings that do not map cleanly to the original report layout.
findings.evidence:
HTML uses "

Active Reported Payment Error Rates

" and "

STATE-REPORTED SUMMARY DATA

", while the PDF is a two-panel report layout without clear heading hierarchy. The HTML also contains nested
elements, which indicates structural issues.

findings.description: The HTML introduces conversion artifacts and minor textual noise that are not part of the document content.
findings.evidence: HTML includes a trailing standalone paragraph "0.00" at the end of the second section. In the PDF, this appears as a small isolated number at the bottom-right margin rather than a normal paragraph in the content flow.
